---
title: "Lab11"
author: "Vandana Brahmasa"
date: "2025-04-24"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

 Part I
 In Part I, we will study return predictability using monthly returns.
 a. We will again use the stock market data set used in Robert Shiller’s book, Irrational Exuberance. Repeat
 the steps in the previous lab “Expected Return” to import the data:
 First, copy, paste, and run the following code chunk to define the load_data function:
```{r}
library(quantmod)
load_data <- function(data_file){
 load(url(paste0(r'{https://web.ics.purdue.edu/~zeng135/}', data_file, '.rdata')),
 envir = .GlobalEnv)}
load_data("Shiller")
head(Shiller)
```
  Then calculate the returns on the S&P composite index.
```{r}
r <- (Shiller$Price + Shiller$Dividend/12)/ lag(Shiller$Price) - 1
head(r)
```
   b. Recall that the acf function can be used to calculate and plot the autocorrelations of a time series. Use acf to study the autocorrelation structure of the returns. What is your observation?
```{r}
sp_returns <- na.omit(r)
acf(sp_returns, main="Autocorrelations of S&P returns")
```
 c. Compute the autocorrelation of the returns for lag of 1 month.
```{r}
acf(sp_returns, lag.max= 1, plot= FALSE)
```
  d. Regress current month’s return on the previous month’s return. Do you see evidence of return predictability?
 Why?
```{r}
current <- r[-1] #current returns first value removed
 
previous <- r[-length(r)] #previous month's returns (lagged)
 
model <- lm (current ~ previous)
summary(model)
```
  In Part II, we will study return predictability using annual returns.
 a. Robert Shiller provides annual series of the data set at:
 http://www.econ.yale.edu/~shiller/data/chapt26.xlsx
 Download the Excel file and and take a look at its contents.
 b. I have downloaded the annual data and converted to a data frame, and saved it as an R data file online.
 Call the load_data function (the same function used in Part I) to load the annual data:
```{r}
load_data("Shiller_annual")
```
 c. Check the head of the data frame data. The meaning of each column can be found in the corresponding
 Excel file. For example, the p column is the S&P Composite Stock Price Index, and the div column contains
 dividends accruing to the index for the whole year.
```{r}
load_data <- function(data_file){
 load(url(paste0(r'{https://web.ics.purdue.edu/~zeng135/}', data_file, '.rdata')),
 envir = .GlobalEnv)}
load_data("Shiller_annual")
head(Shiller_data_annual)
```
  d. In order to manipulate the time series data, convert the data frame into an xts object, using the date
 column for the time stamp. Name the xts object data_xts. Plot the price
```{r}
library(xts)
###convert shiller data to Date format:
Shiller_data_annual$date <- as.Date(paste0(Shiller_data_annual$date, "-01-01"))
###cxts object
data_xts <- xts(Shiller_data_annual[, -1], order.by = Shiller_data_annual$date)
# Step 4: Plot the S&P Composite Price Index (column 'p')
plot(data_xts$p, main = "S&P Composite Index Price (Annual)", 
     ylab = "Price", xlab = "Year", col = "blue")
```
